questions.question: What is the key difference between the charity model, service model, and service learning model?

options.text: They are all identical approaches to helping
Charity is one-way giving; service adds relationship; service learning adds structured reflection, reciprocity, and critical examination of systems
Only service learning is ever appropriate
Charity is best because it requires less commitment

questions.explanation: The three models represent increasing depth: Charity is one-way giving (appropriate for emergencies, immediate needs), Service adds ongoing relationship and mutual respect (appropriate for sustained volunteering), and Service Learning adds structured reflection, reciprocity, critical examination of power and systems, and transformation of both server and served (appropriate for addressing root causes with sustained engagement). All three have a place—the key is matching the model to the situation. Emergency relief needs charity. Systemic change needs service learning.

questions.question: According to the lesson, what is the FIRST principle of effective service?

options.text: Raise as much money as possible
Start with community-identified needs, not assumed needs—always ask, never assume
Implement the solution you think is best
Focus only on immediate symptoms, not root causes

questions.explanation: The foundational principle is: Communities know their own needs better than outsiders. Always ask, never assume. This means conducting listening sessions, following community leadership, funding community-identified priorities, and being willing to hear 'That's not what we need.' The history of charity is full of mismatched solutions (winter coats to tropical climates, infrastructure nobody wanted) because helpers assumed rather than asked. Starting with community voices prevents wasted resources and respects community expertise and self-determination.

questions.question: What is 'saviorism' or the 'white savior complex'?

options.text: A positive approach to helping that emphasizes expertise
Believing you're rescuing helpless people, centering your heroism over community agency—often privileged outsiders imposing solutions communities didn't ask for
Any type of cross-cultural service
A theoretical concept with no real-world application

questions.explanation: Saviorism is believing you're rescuing helpless people while centering your own heroism over community agency. It often involves outsiders (especially privileged outsiders based on race, class, nationality) imposing 'solutions' on communities who didn't ask for help, treating people as objects to be saved rather than agents with knowledge and capacity. The 'white savior complex' specifically describes this dynamic when white people from privileged backgrounds enter communities of color. It's harmful because it erodes community agency, creates dependency, and centers the helper's feelings and identity rather than genuine community benefit.

questions.question: What does it mean to work WITH communities rather than FOR them?

options.text: Making decisions about what communities need without their input
Shared decision-making, amplifying community voices, building capacity, asking 'How can I support what you're doing?' vs 'Let me do this for you'
Doing everything yourself to ensure quality
It means the same thing as working for them

questions.explanation: Working WITH communities means partnership, not paternalism: shared decision-making and leadership, amplifying community voices rather than speaking for them, building community capacity rather than creating dependency, and asking 'How can I support what you're already doing?' rather than 'Let me do this for you.' The difference is profound: working FOR communities creates dependency and erodes agency (when you leave, they're weaker). Working WITH communities builds power and sustainability (when you leave, they're stronger). It requires sharing power, following community leadership, and recognizing community members as experts on their own lives.

questions.question: Why is structured reflection essential to service learning (vs. service alone)?

options.text: Reflection isn't important; only action matters
Reflection transforms experience into learning—examining assumptions, understanding systems and power, identifying what worked/didn't, and improving future practice
Reflection is only for academic credit
Reflection makes you feel better about yourself

questions.explanation: Reflection is what transforms service into service LEARNING. Without reflection, you might repeat the same mistakes, miss opportunities to examine power dynamics and your own assumptions, or fail to learn from the community. Critical reflection asks: What did you learn about systems? Power? Your own privilege or blind spots? The community's strengths? What assumptions were challenged? What will you do differently next time? This metacognitive process—thinking about your thinking and experience—is what creates growth and prevents perpetuating harm through well-intentioned but unexamined service. The Plan-Act-Reflect-Improve cycle makes service iterative and responsive rather than static.
